segments.s367-g57: پسر داشتی is a simple past clause: داشتی is the 2nd‑person singular past of داشتن, so “you had a son.” یک گرانمایه مرد is an appositive noun phrase describing that son; گرانمایه (an adjective) precedes مرد (man) here as a poetic word order, and یک marks indefiniteness.
segments.s367-g58: جهاندیده is an adjectival epithet meaning “worldly‑wise” modifying the man; و simply links two descriptors. دیده is a past participle used adjectivally (“having seen / experienced”), and هر گرم و سرد is a fixed antithetical pair (“every heat and cold”) meaning all conditions — together yielding “who had experienced every hot and cold.”
segments.s367-g59: سواری جهاندیده is a noun plus adjective: سواری “rider/horseman” qualified by جهاندیده “worldly‑wise.” نامش is name + 3rd‑person possessive clitic (“his name”), and the copula is omitted before the name کهرم, so the sequence reads “a worldly‑wise horseman — his name Kahram.”
segments.s367-g60: رسیده is a past participle/preterite used intransitively here to mean “had come/beenfall(ed).” بسی is an adverbial intensifier (“much/many”), بر سرش is a locative phrase literally “upon his head” meaning “to him,” and سرد و گرم names the paired hardships “cold and heat” — together: many cold and hot things had befallen him.
segments.s367-g61: مران پور is a name‑like sequence (مران + پور); پور here functions as the name‑element (part of a compound/patronymic). خود را is the accusative reflexive emphasizing that he is the object (“himself”), را is the definite/object marker, and سپهدار کرد is a verb phrase “made/appointed (him) sepahdar” with کرد as the past tense of کردن (“to make/do”).
segments.s367-g62: بران is an archaic/poetic adverbial meaning “thereupon” or “on that,” linking the next action to the prior one. لشکر is “army,” گشن here functions as a proper name (the person being appointed) rather than its lexical senses, سالار کرد is “made (him) commander” with سالار = commander and کرد = ‘made’. The direct object (the person) is contextually understood.
segments.s367-g1: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning 'when'. جاماسپ گفت is a simple past clause 'Jamasp said' with subject before the past verb گفت; the following words form the quoted content: این سپیده دمید — این (this) + سپیده (dawn) + دمید (past of دمیدن used here to mean 'dawned/rose'). The whole segment therefore reads 'When Jamasp said, "This dawn has risen."'
segments.s367-g2: فروغ ستاره is a genitive phrase 'the radiance of the star'. بشد is the past/copular sense 'it became' linking to the predicate ناپدید 'vanished, gone' — together 'the star's radiance became vanished'. Note the classical/poetic past form بشد.
segments.s367-g3: سپه را uses را as the definite/accusative marker ('the army' as direct object). به هامون is the destination 'to/into the plain.' فرود آورید is a compound verb meaning 'brought down / caused to descend' (classical past/plural form); word order places the object before the verb, common in epic style.
segments.s367-g4: بزد is the past tense 'he/they struck'. کوس is the direct object 'war-drum', and بر پیل 'upon the elephant' is a prepositional phrase specifying where/on whom the drum was struck. و links the next clause لشکر کشید, where کشید is the past of کشیدن meaning 'to draw/deploy' (i.e. 'drew up the army').
segments.s367-g5: وزانجا is a compound of و + زانجا meaning 'and from there', linking the motion to the previous place. خرامید is the past tense 'he strode/proudly advanced'. تا رزمگاه uses تا as a marker of endpoint 'as far as / to the battlefield.'
segments.s367-g6: فرود آورید is again the compound 'caused to alight / deployed' (past), followed by the object آن گزیده سپاه 'that chosen army' where آن is the demonstrative, گزیده 'chosen/elite' modifies سپاه (army). The verb governs the whole noun phrase as its object.
segments.s367-g7: به گاهی is a temporal prepositional phrase 'at a time / at the hour'. که introduces a subordinate/time clause 'when'. باد سپیده دمان is a poetic nominal group 'the wind/breeze of the dawn's hem' (باد = breeze/wind; سپیده دمان = 'the dawn's skirt' i.e. early morning), so together 'when the morning breeze...'
segments.s367-g8: به کاخ آرد is 'brings to the palace' — آرد is the classical/present(ive) 3rd singular of آوردن used in narrative to mean 'brings'. از باغ بوی گلان is the source phrase 'the scent of flowers from the garden' (از باغ = from the garden; بوی گلان = scent of flowers), with poetic word order placing the source before the noun phrase.
segments.s367-g33: پس is a sentence adverb meaning “then” introducing what follows. پشت لشکر is a two‑word noun phrase (پشت = ‘rear/behind’, لشکر = ‘army’) meaning “the rear of the army”; Persian often places the part (پشت) before the possessor (لشکر) without an ezafe. به + noun + داد is the verb construction ‘gave/assigned to X’: here به introduces the recipient/holder and داد is the past tense verb “gave/placed/assigned.”
segments.s367-g34: This noun cluster names the person who receives the charge. چراغ here is a poetic epithet meaning “lamp/bright one” or “beacon” (figurative epithet for a distinguished person). سپهدار is a title/office (‘spahbed, commander’). خسرو نژاد is a compound name/epithet: خسرو (the name Khosrow) + نژاد (‘lineage, of the race of’). Together they identify the man to whom the rear was given.
segments.s367-g35: چو is a temporal conjunction here = “when.” لشکر بیاراست is an action clause: بیاراست (past/ perfective sense ‘arrayed/adorned’) governs لشکر as its object/undergoer: ‘he arrayed the army.’ و links the two verbs; بر شد is a light/compound verb (بر + شد) meaning ‘rose/ascended/marched up’ (شد is the past 3rd‑singular inchoative/auxiliary here). به کوه marks the direction/goal “onto the mountain.”
segments.s367-g36: This is a two‑clause predicate describing his inner state. غمی گشته literally ‘has become grief’ = ‘was filled with grief’; گشته is a perfect/inchoative participle here meaning ‘became/was made.’ از رنج is the cause phrase ‘from/out of suffering/hardship.’ و links to the next result clause گشته ستوه where the second گشته again means ‘became’ and ستوه is the adjective ‘weary/overcome.’
segments.s367-g37: نشست is the simple past ‘he sat (down).’ از بر is a prepositional combination here meaning ‘away from/aside from (the) …’ — بر governs the following noun as a locative complement. خوب تابنده گاه is a descriptive noun phrase: خوب ‘beautiful/fair,’ تابنده ‘shining/resplendent,’ گاه here functions as ‘place/seat’ (poetic use). So the whole segment says he sat down away from that bright, beautiful place.
segments.s367-g38: همی is an older/adverbial particle meaning ‘continually/kept on.’ کرد is the light/ordinary past verb ‘did/made’ which here combines with نگاه (a verbal noun) to mean ‘kept looking.’ زانجا ‘from there/thence’ marks the vantage point, به لشکر is the goal ‘toward the army,’ and نگاه is the object ‘gaze/view.’ The literal structure is ‘kept (doing) — from there toward the army (a) look,’ i.e. ‘kept looking from there at the army.’
segments.s367-g39: پس = ‘then.’ ارجاسپ is a personal name (Arjasp) and شاه is the title ‘king,’ here in apposition: ‘Arjasp the king.’ دلیران is the plural noun ‘the brave ones/warriors.’ چین is used poetically to mean ‘folds/rows’ or ‘ranks’ (derived sense of ‘fold’) and qualifies how the brave were arranged — i.e. ‘arrayed in ranks.’ Word order: title/name + appositive + description.
segments.s367-g40: بیاراست is the verb ‘arrayed/put in order’ (past). لشکرش is the possessed noun ‘his army’ (لشکر + possessive ـش). را is the Persian definite/direct‑object marker attaching to لشکرش to mark it as the object of بیاراست. همچنین is an adverb ‘likewise/similarly,’ modifying the verb phrase and indicating that he arranged his army in the same fashion. The clause thus says he arrayed his army in the same way.
segments.s367-g41: جدا کرد is a past‑tense verbal phrase: جدا (literally “apart”) plus کرد (“did/made”) yields “separated” or “detached.” از marks the source (‘from’). خلخی here names the group or people (a proper name in context); the normal order is verb + source phrase following it.
segments.s367-g42: This is a noun phrase describing those detached: جهان آزموده is a compound adjective meaning “tried/experienced in the world” (literally “world‑tested”). نبرده is negative perfect/periphrastic usage meaning “not taken/never overcome” modifying سوار (“rider/horseman”); the three words together form a single descriptive phrase (world‑tested, unbeaten horsemen).
segments.s367-g43: فرستادشان is the past verb 'he sent' with the object pronoun ‑شان attached (‘sent them’). سوی is the preposition ‘toward’; آن is the demonstrative ‘that’; بیدرفش is a descriptive/epithet meaning ‘without a banner’ (here functioning as a noun or epithet for a person). Word order places the verb before the directional phrase in this narrative line.
segments.s367-g44: که introduces a relative clause modifying the preceding epithet: 'who/which.' کوس مهین داشت is a simple clause: کوس (war‑drum) + مهین (an honorific adjective meaning ‘mighty/majestic’) + داشت (had). و links the two possessions; رنگین درفش places the adjective رنگین (‘colorful’) before the noun درفش (‘banner/standard’) in this poetic ordering.
segments.s367-g45: بدو is an indirect object pronoun phrase meaning 'to him' (preverbal here). داد is the past verb 'gave.' یک دست is the object 'one hand/unit' (a military detachment), and زان لشکرش means 'from that army' (زان = 'from that'; لشکرش = 'his/that army' with possessive suffix). The object phrase precedes or accompanies the verb in typical narrative word order.
segments.s367-g46: که begins a relative clause modifying 'یک دست': شیر ژیان نامدی is presented as a name or epithet (literally 'lion of life' + a name), and همبرش = همبر + ‑ش is a noun meaning 'companion' or 'fellow' with the possessive suffix referring back to the detachment ('its companion'). The clause therefore says which figure was the detachment's companion/leader.
segments.s367-g47: دگر دست را داد is 'he gave the other/another detachment' (دگر = 'other', دست = 'detachment/unit', را = definite object marker, داد = 'gave'). بر گرگسار is the beneficiary/target phrase 'to Gorgsār' (بر + name); in narrative Persian the recipient can follow the verb or be introduced by بر.
segments.s367-g48: بدادش here functions as a verbal phrase 'he gave him' with the enclitic ‑ش ('to him'); سوار گزین is 'chosen/selected riders' (noun + adjective), and صدهزار is the numeral 'one hundred thousand' specifying quantity. The sequence is a compact poetic ordering listing what was given: chosen horsemen numbering a hundred thousand.
segments.s367-g25: This chunk contains the verbal idea + agent + object-noun in a compressed, archaic order. بدادش is an older/poetic fused form: داد (gave) with a clitic -ش (to him/it); here the form is irregular in the lemma list, so treat it as ‘gave (to him)’. جهاندار is the agent 'the sovereign, world-ruler' that carries out the giving. پنجه functions metaphorically as 'grip/command/contingent' (a military 'hand' or 'clutch') and immediately quantifies the force that is being handed over.
segments.s367-g26: This finishes the object phrase: سوار گزیده is a postpositive adjectival phrase 'chosen horsemen/riders' (گزیده = selected/chosen modifying سوار). به + اسفندیار marks the ultimate recipient 'to Esfandiyar' (preposition + proper name). Word order is Verb + object phrase earlier, with the recipient at the end.
segments.s367-g27: بدو داد is 'gave to him' (بدو = 'to him' placed before the verb for emphasis). یک دست = 'one hand' i.e. 'one detachment/division' (دست used metaphorically for a unit). زان لشکرش = 'of that army' (زان = 'from that/of that'), so the whole segment means 'he gave him one detachment from that army.'
segments.s367-g28: که introduces a relative clause describing that detachment. شیری دلش بود is literal 'its/whose heart was leonine' (شیری modifies دل; the clitic ش on دل = 'its/his'). و links a second quality: پیلی برش is terse/poetic and literally 'an elephant upon it' or 'it had an elephant'; the exact function of برش is unclear in the lemma list, but here it marks something 'on/with it' — the sense is ‘and it carried/had an elephant’.
segments.s367-g29: دگر دست لشکرش = 'the other/another hand (division) of his army' (دگر = 'other/another'); را is the object marker attached to the noun phrase (marks the definite direct object). همچنان is an adverb 'in the same way / likewise' modifying the following verb.
segments.s367-g30: برآراست is a past-tense, third-person singular verb meaning 'he arrayed/arranged/adorned' (برـ + آراخت). از introduces the source/material: 'from' or 'of' (از شیر دل سرکشان = 'from lion‑hearted ...'). شیر دل is a compound 'lion-hearted' (شیر + دل), and سرکشان is the group described (the lemma given is off; here it functions as 'the bold/proud/rowdy ones' or 'warriors').
segments.s367-g31: به marks the indirect object 'to/into (the charge of)'; گرد here is part of a terse phrase 'گرد گرامی' that functions as the person or office receiving the army (the exact lexical identity of گرد in the provided senses is uncertain, so treat it as a role/title), and گرامی is an epithet 'honored, noble.' سپرد is the past tense 'he entrusted/handed over.' آن سپاه is the direct object 'that army' (آن = 'that', سپاه = 'army').
segments.s367-g32: که introduces a relative/clausal description of the person or force just entrusted: 'who was شیر جهان' = 'the lion of the world' (a standard epithet meaning the bravest/most formidable), بود is past 'was', و links 'and', همتای شاه = 'the equal/peer of the king' (همتای = counterpart). The clause qualifies the recipient named in the previous segment.
segments.s367-g9: فرستاده is a noun 'messenger' (here the agent). The phrase has a verb-less, terse poetic order where the action 'went/was sent' is implied by context; هر سوی دیده‌بان = 'to each (every) sentinel/direction of the sentinels' (هر modifies سوی, and سوی governs دیده‌بان). The item بد here does not match the provided senses list and appears to be an irregular/archaic element (see note below) so the phrase is best read as 'a messenger (was sent) to every sentinel.'
segments.s367-g10: چنانچون introduces a simile 'just as/like'; بود is the past copula 'was' (here stating a past habitual fact); رسم is 'custom' and آزادگان 'the free/nobles' — together: 'just as was the custom of the free.' Word order is typical poetic: simile marker + copula + noun phrase.
segments.s367-g11: بیامد is a past-tense verb 'came' placed before its subject سواری ('a rider/horseman'), which is a common narrative order in older Persian. و links the two actions; گفتا is an archaic past form 'said'; به شاه is the indirect object 'to the king.'
segments.s367-g12: که introduces reported speech ('that' / 'saying that'); شاها is a vocative 'O king!' inserted in the reported clause; به نزدیکی آمد سپاه is a poetic word order where the locative phrase 'به نزدیکی' ('near') precedes آمد ('came') and the subject سپاه ('the army') appears after the verb — together: 'that, O king, an army has come near.'
segments.s367-g13: سپاهیست is a copular/nominal clause meaning 'it is an army' (سپاهی + است contracted). ای شهریار زمین is a vocative address 'O lord/king of the land' (ای marks direct address; شهریار + زمین forms the title). The clause names and addresses the addressee after the nominal statement.
segments.s367-g14: که starts a relative/explanatory clause modifying 'army'; هرگز چنان is 'never such' (هرگز = 'never', چنان = 'such'); نامد is an archaic/poetic form here meaning 'came' (historically written without the negative prefix n), so the clause reads 'such never came'; از ترک و چین = 'from Turk and China' (listing origins). The word order is inverted for poetical effect.
segments.s367-g15: به نزدیکی ما is a locative phrase 'to/near us' (به + نزدیکی + pronoun ما). فرود آمدند is a compound past verb 'they alighted/descended/arrived' (فرود + آمدند), with آمدند as the past plural verb — together: 'they came down/approached near us.'
segments.s367-g16: به + list of places 'کوه و در و دشت' = 'on mountain(s), in the pass/gate (here meaning pass or mountain-gate), and on the plain'; خیمه زدند is an idiom 'they pitched tents' where زدند (past of زدن) is used in the sense 'set up/strike [tents/pegs]'. The verb is past plural, matching the previously mentioned actors.
segments.s367-g49: میان‌گاه is an adverbial ('in the midst'/'midway') placed front for emphasis. لشکرش را is the definite object (لشکر + enclitic possessive -ش, plus object marker را) preparing for the verb that follows; همچنین ('likewise/similarly') links this action to the previous one — it qualifies the manner in which he arranges the host.
segments.s367-g50: سپاهی here is a noun 'a host/army' (not adjectival). بیاراست is a past/preterite verbal form ('arrayed/arranged/adorned') taking لشکرش را as its object. خوب و گزین are adjectival modifiers: خوب 'good/fine' and گزین 'select/chosen' ( گزین used adjectivally). Word order is poetic but the clause reads: he arranged (بیاراست) a fine and select host.
segments.s367-g51: بدادش is the past verb 'داد' plus enclitic object pronoun -ش ('gave him') and functions as the main verb 'he gave him'. بدان is an instrumental/benefactive 'by/with that' (in classical Persian written together), followed by جادوی خویش 'the magic/sorcery of his' (جادوی = magic; خویش = 'his own', realized here as خوی + enclitic ش). کام is the direct object 'desire/wish' — so: 'he gave him, by that magic of his, his desire.'
segments.s367-g52: کجا here functions in a relative/indefinite sense 'where/whenever/wherever' introducing the circumstance; نام is 'name'; خواست is past 'he wanted/asked for'. The clause says that whenever/wherever he sought a name (کجا نام خواست), هزارانش نام (lit. 'thousands of it names') indicates that thousands of names came/appeared — i.e. 'thousands of names' were produced. The coordination و links the two parts.
segments.s367-g53: This is a compound subject: خود 'he himself' joined by و plus صدهزاران سواران 'a hundred thousand horsemen'. گرد here conveys the sense 'together/around' (they gathered around). The sequence lists who assembled before the predicate in the next segment.
segments.s367-g54: نموده is the perfect/past participial form of نمودن ('made/caused/formed') used here as the verbal predicate 'they made/did', with همه 'all' as its scope. در جهان دستبرد is a prepositional phrase 'plundering in the world' (دستبرد = plunder/theft/raid). Together: '...assembled and made plunder through the whole world.'
segments.s367-g55: نگاهش is نگاه + enclitic -ش 'his gaze'. همی داشت is a classical imperfective construction ('was keeping' / 'kept on having') with همی marking continuous/habitual aspect and داشت serving as the past imperfect auxiliary. پشت سپاه is the locative 'at/over the rear of the army' — so: 'He kept his gaze on the army's rear.'
segments.s367-g56: همی کرد is another habitual/imperfective turn ('he kept doing/made repeatedly'); here کرد 'to do/make' combines with همی to yield 'kept turning/directing'. هر سوی لشکر is the phrase 'every direction/side of the army' (هر سوی = every side toward), and نگاه at the end is the object 'a glance/gaze' (poetic word order: object placed sentence-finally). Thus: 'he kept turning his gaze to every side of the army.'
segments.s367-g17: سپهدارشان is سپهدار + the possessive clitic -شان, 'their commander'. It appears as the subject before the verb. دیده‌بان is a compound noun 'lookout/sentinel' serving as the direct object. برگزید is a simple past (3rd sg.) verb 'chose/selected' placed at the end, following the typical Persian SOV order.
segments.s367-g18: فرستاد is a past tense verb 'he sent' (3rd singular). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. دیده به دیده is an idiomatic prepositional phrase 'eye to eye / sight to sight' (دیده = 'eye', به = 'to'), and رسید is the past intransitive 'reached' used here idiomatically to mean 'met / saw one another'.
segments.s367-g19: پس is a discourse adverb 'then/thereupon'. آزاده is an epithet 'noble' modifying the name گشتاسپ (Gushtasp). شاه is the title 'king' following the name, and دلیر is an adjective 'brave' modifying شاه; together they form a nominal phrase that serves as the subject of the next verb.
segments.s367-g20: سپهبدش is سپهبد + possessive clitic -ش 'his spahbed'. را is the definite/direct-object marker attached to that phrase. خواند here is past tense 'called/summoned' (i.e. he summoned). The following words فرخ زریر give the name of the person summoned (name in apposition to the object).
segments.s367-g21: درفشی is the object 'a banner/standard' (here 'a banner'). بدו is the prepositional pronoun 'to him'. داد is the past tense 'gave'. و connects clauses, and گفتا is an older/formal past 'he said'. بتاز is an imperative formed with the verbal stem تاز and the prefix ب-: here an order 'Charge!' or 'Rush/attack!'.
segments.s367-g22: This is a sequence of imperatives/commands. بیارای here functions as an instruction (verb form) 'array/prepare' (note: the provided sense list doesn't match this verbal use, so sense is marked -1). پیلان is the plural 'elephants' (object). و links the two commands. لشکر is 'army' (object), and بساز is an imperative 'make/assemble/form' (2nd‑person command). Object(s) appear before the verbs, which is normal in Persian imperative commands.
segments.s367-g23: سپهبد is the noun 'spahbed/commander'. بشد is an archaic past form of شدن here meaning 'became / took up (his post)' or simply 'so it was'; it is 3rd person past. لشکرش 'his army' is the object/affected noun, and راست کرد is a semi‑verbal combination: راست 'straight/in order' plus the light verb کرد 'made/did' → 'set straight / put in order.' The verb cluster comes at the end, consistent with Persian SOV order.
segments.s367-g24: همی is an adverb often used in epic Persian to indicate immediate or continuous action ('then/just'). رزم سالار is a compound noun 'battle‑commander / war‑chief' (subject). چین here denotes an arranged line/folding → 'array/formation' (the military 'rank/array'), and خواست کرد is a light‑verb construction where خواست (wanted/ordered) plus کرد functions together as 'ordered/commanded' (past). The sequence thus means 'the battle‑chief ordered the formation/array.'

sentences.s367-s29: You had a son, a noble man — worldly‑wise and who had seen every heat and cold.
sentences.s367-s30: A worldly‑wise rider, his name Kahram; many heats and colds had befallen him.
sentences.s367-s31: Maran‑pur made himself sepahdar, and thereupon made Gashn the army’s commander.
sentences.s367-s1: When Jamasp said, "The dawn has risen," the star's radiance vanished.
sentences.s367-s2: They brought the army down onto the plain; they struck the war-drum on an elephant and drew up the host.
sentences.s367-s3: And from there he strode proudly to the battlefield; he deployed that chosen army.
sentences.s367-s4: At the hour when the dawn-breeze brings to the palace from the garden the scent of flowers.
sentences.s367-s17: Then he put the rear of the army in charge of Bostur — the brilliant spahbed of Khosrow's line.
sentences.s367-s18: When he arrayed the army and advanced up onto the mountain, he was filled with grief from hardship and became weary.
sentences.s367-s19: He sat down apart from the shining, fair place, and from there he kept gazing at the army.
sentences.s367-s20: Then Arjasp, the king, arrayed his brave men in ranks and likewise set his army in order.
sentences.s367-s21: He detached thirty thousand from the Khalakhi — battle‑tested horsemen who had never been beaten.
sentences.s367-s22: He sent them toward that bannerless one, who bore the mighty drum and a colorful standard.
sentences.s367-s23: He gave him one detachment from that army, whose companion was Namdi, the Lion‑Zhiyan.
sentences.s367-s24: He gave another detachment to Gorgsār; he gave him a hundred thousand chosen horsemen.
sentences.s367-s13: The sovereign gave Esfandiyar the command of a thousand chosen horsemen.
sentences.s367-s14: He gave him one detachment of that army — one whose heart was of a lion and which had an elephant.
sentences.s367-s15: He similarly arrayed the other division of his army, drawn from lion‑hearted (bold) men.
sentences.s367-s16: He entrusted that army to the honored commander, who was the lion of the world and the equal of the king.
sentences.s367-s5: A messenger went to each of the sentinels, just as was the custom of free men.
sentences.s367-s6: A horseman came and said to the king, 'O king, an army has come near.'
sentences.s367-s7: It is an army, O sovereign of the land, such as never before came from Turk and China.
sentences.s367-s8: They came near us; on mountain, at pass/gate, and on the plain they pitched their tents.
sentences.s367-s25: In the midst he likewise arrayed his army — a fine and select host.
sentences.s367-s26: By that sorcery he granted him his desire; wherever he asked for a name, thousands of names (were given).
sentences.s367-s27: He himself and a hundred thousand horsemen gathered together and made plunder throughout the world.
sentences.s367-s28: He kept his gaze on the army's rear; he continually cast his look to every side of the host.
sentences.s367-s9: Their commander chose a lookout, sent him, and sight met sight (they saw one another).
sentences.s367-s10: Then the noble Gushtasp, the brave king, summoned his spahbed, Farrokh‑Zarir.
sentences.s367-s11: He gave him a banner and said, 'Charge! Array the elephants and form the army.'
sentences.s367-s12: The spahbed took his post and set his army in order; the war‑chief ordered the battle array.
